coredata.dc:title: Ag-based semiconductor photocatalysts in environmental purification
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jiade
Fang, Wen
Yu, Changlin
Zhou, Wanqin
zhu, Lihua
Xie, Yu
coredata.dc:description: Over the past decades, with the fast development of global industrial development, various organic pollutants discharged in water have become a major source of environmental pollution in waste fields. Photocatalysis, as green and environmentally friendly technology, has attracted much attention in pollutants degradation due to its efficient degradation rate. However, the practical application of traditional semiconductor photocatalysts, e.g. TiO2, ZnO, is limited by their weak visible light adsorption due to their wide band gaps. Nowadays, the study in photocatalysts focuses on new and narrow band gap semiconductors. Among them, Ag-based semiconductors as promising visible light-driven photocatalysts have aroused much interesting due to their strong visible light responsibility. Most of Ag-based semiconductors could exhibit high initial photocatalytic activity. But they easy suffer from poor stability because of photochemical corrosion. Design heterojunction, increasing specific surface area, enriching pore structure, regulating morphology, controlling crystal facets, and producing plasmonic effects were considered as the effective strategies to improve the photocatalytic performance of Ag-based photocatalyts. Moreover, combining the superior properties of carbon materials (e.g. carbon quantum dots, carbon nano-tube, carbon nanofibers, graphene) with Ag-based semiconductor could produce high efficient composite photocatalyts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Semiconductor
Visible light
Ag-based photocatalysts

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Simple Ag-based compounds and their composites
Some strategies in design Ag-based semiconductor photocatalysts with high performance
Morphology control of Ag-based semiconductor photocatalysts

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The catalytic mechanism schematic diagram of Ag/AgBr [44].
The crystal structure diagram of Ag3PO4 [52].
(a) SEM and (b) TEM images of the porous Ag3PO4 microcubes [62].
Schematic descriptions for the role of p−n heterojunction in enhancing the separation efficiency of e−−h+ pairs [76].
Cycling runs in photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange in the presence of Ag2CO3 or 20% AgI/Ag2CO3 under visible-light irradiation [36].
SEM images of samples prepared by (a) C12MimCl and (b) C16MimCl for 4h at 160°C. [91].

SEM images of the Ag2O microcrystals synthesized with various morphologies: (a) cubes with exposed {100} facets; (b) octahedra with exposed {111} facets; (c) rhombic dodecahedra with exposed {110} facets; (d) polyhedra with 18 faces with exposed {100} and {110} facets; (e) rhombicuboctahedra with exposed {100}, {110} and {111} facets [91].
HRTEM image of (a) Ag−AgBr/MAP and (b) Ag−AgBr/MA [97].
(a) SEM and (b) HRTEM images of CQDs/Ag3PO4 composite photocatalyst [104].
The first-order reaction rate constant of the samples in degradation of MO [36].
Photocatalytic properties of Ag-based heterojunction photocatalyst.

row.entry: 3.6 and 20 times higher than Ag2CO3 and AgI
Degrading MO under UV and visible light
TiO2(0.19h−1, 0.07h−1), Ag2CO3(0.28h−1, 0.14h−1), TiO2(1%)/Ag2CO3 (1.12h−1, 0.24h−1) under UV and visible light, respectively
6, 4 times under UV and 3.4, 1.7 times under visible higher than TiO2 and Ag2CO3
Degradation of RhB under visible light
59 times that of BiOCl and 12 times that of Ag/AgCl
5.5 and 10 times higher than Ag3PO4 and BiOCl
Degrading RhB and phenol under visible light
3.81 and 5.24 times higher than BiOBr and Ag3PO4
Degrading MO and phenol under visible light
TiO2/Ag3PO4/Ag (1.328h−1 for MO and 0.242h−1 for phenol), Ag3PO4 (0.387h−1 for MO and 0.158h−1 for phenol)
3.43 and 1.53 times higher than pure Ag3PO4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic properties of special morphology Ag-based photocatalyst.

row.entry: Photocatalyst with special morphology or crystal
Ag3PO4 crystals (branch, tetrapod, nanorod, triangular prism)
Degrading MB or RhB under visible light

Branched Ag3PO4 (0.152min−1),
tetrapod Ag3PO4 (0.035min−1),
nanorod-shaped Ag3PO4 (0.074min−1),
triangular-prism-shaped Ag3PO4 (0.033min−1),
irregular spherical Ag3PO4 (0.055min−1)
Branched Ag3PO4 are 2.8 and 4 times ofthat of irregular spherical Ag3PO4
Degrading RhB and MB under visible light (420<λ)
Nanoparticles, trisoctahedrons, tetrahedrons and tetrapods are 22, 3.5, 2.6 and 2.3 times of that of commercial TiO2 catalyst
Irregular AgBr particles (8min), AgBr nanoplates (2min) for 100% degradation rate
AgBr nanoplates is four times of irregular AgBr particles

head.ce:title: Ag-based semiconductor photocatalysts in environmental purification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Metallurgy and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou 341000, Jiangxi Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Metallurgy and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment Engineering and biology Engineering, Guangdong University of Petrochemical Technology, Maoming, 525000 Guangdong Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environment Engineering and biology Engineering, Guangdong University of Petrochemical Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, 350002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, Fuzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, Jiangxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environment and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Metallurgy and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou 341000, Jiangxi Province, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ag-based semiconductors as promising visible light-driven photocatalysts have aroused much interesting due to their strong visible light responsibility. Formation of heterojunction could largely promote the electron/hole pair separation, resulting in highly photocatalytic activity and stability.

Over the past decades, with the fast development of global industrial development, various organic pollutants discharged in water have become a major source of environmental pollution in waste fields. Photocatalysis, as green and environmentally friendly technology, has attracted much attention in pollutants degradation due to its efficient degradation rate. However, the practical application of traditional semiconductor photocatalysts, e.g. TiO2, ZnO, is limited by their weak visible light adsorption due to their wide band gaps. Nowadays, the study in photocatalysts focuses on new and narrow band gap semiconductors. Among them, Ag-based semiconductors as promising visible light-driven photocatalysts have aroused much interesting due to their strong visible light responsibility. Most of Ag-based semiconductors could exhibit high initial photocatalytic activity. But they easy suffer from poor stability because of photochemical corrosion. Design heterojunction, increasing specific surface area, enriching pore structure, regulating morphology, controlling crystal facets, and producing plasmonic effects were considered as the effective strategies to improve the photocatalytic performance of Ag-based photocatalyts. Moreover, combining the superior properties of carbon materials (e.g. carbon quantum dots, carbon nano-tube, carbon nanofibers, graphene) with Ag-based semiconductor could produce high efficient composite photocatalyts.

ce:section.ce:para: At present, the global environmental and energy problems become more and more serious. The photocatalytic technology of semiconductor can convert solar energy into hydrogen and other chemical energy under mild condition. More importantly, in recent years, photocatalysis, as green and environmentally friendly process, has attracted much attention in pollutants degradation due to its rapid decomposition rate, high mineralization efficiency and low toxigenicity, ideally producing CO2 and H2O as the end-products [1–5]. Other applications of photocatalysis mainly include sewage treatment, air purification, solar energy conversion, organic synthesis, and etc. [6–8].

In heterogeneous photocatalytic process, when the energy of the incident light is larger than the band gap energy of semiconductor, the electrons (e) in valence band (VB) were excited to conduction band (CB) and holes (h+) were left in the valence band. On one hand, part of the produced e− and h+ will spread to the surface of the catalyst, inducing the redox reactions with the electron acceptor or electron donor which adsorbed on the surface of the catalyst. On the other hand, most of the photogenerated e− and h+ combine directly, releasing the energy in the form of light and heat, then consumed by themselves. The intensively investigated semiconductor photocatalysis materials contain pure and modified titanium dioxide, zinc oxide, sulfide, nitrogen oxide, composite semiconductors, and etc [9,10]. Among them, TiO2 has become the most study and application of photocatalyst. In photocatalysis, TiO2 has some obvious advantages, such as low price, nontoxicity, strong oxidation ability, and good stability. But TiO2 can only be activated by UV light to generate the electron (e–)–hole (h+) pairs, which trigger next photocatalytic reactions. However, the UV light energy fraction in whole solar light is only about 2−3%. Therefore, TiO2 and other semiconductors with large band gap (>3.1eV) cannot effectively use natural solar light or artificial visible light. To overcome this disadvantage, different methods have been developed, for example, coupling with a small band gap semiconductor [11–14], the addition of transition metals [15,16], and nonmetal elements doping [17,18]. These modifications are expected to extend the light absorption of TiO2 from UV light to visible light region. On the other side, it is urgent to develop new type of visible light-driven photocatalysts [19–22]. Up to now, different visible light photocatalysts have been developed, such as gold nanoclusters (Au25(SR)18/TiO2)[23], TiO2-x N x
[24], Rh(Pd, Pt)/BiOX(Cl, Br, I)[25], g-C3N4 [26], supported plasmonic noble metal (e.g. gold, silver) nanoparticles (NPs) [27], and etc.

Recent studies found that most of the Ag-based semiconductors could exhibit strong visible light absorption and obvious visible light catalytic activity due to their narrow band gap energy [28–30]. This paper mainly demonstrated the recent progresses in design and development of the typical Ag-based photocatalysts (including simple silver compounds, Ag-based heterojunctions, Ag-based solid solutions, loaded Ag-based semiconductors, carbon materials coupled Ag-based semiconductors, and etc.) and their applications in decomposing the typical organic pollutants. Some strategies for improving the photocatalytic stability and activity were summarized and analyzed.

Nowadays, there are different kinds of reported Ag-based semiconductors, e.g. Ag2O, Ag2S, AgX(Cl, Br, I), Ag3AsO4, AgPO4, AgAlO2, and etc. The band gap energy (E g), the potentials of the conduct band (CB) and valence band (VB) of some typical Ag-based semiconductors are listed in Table 1 . Basically, all Ag-based semiconductors are responsive in the visible light irradiation due to their small band gap energy (<3.0eV). Even less band gap energies (<2.0eV) were observed over AgCl, Ag2O and Ag2S. It should be noted that the photocatalytic performance of semiconductor is affected not only by its band gap, but also by the composition, crystal structure, morphology, and etc. Due to their strong visible light absorption, great efforts have been devoted to the design, preparation and characterization of Ag-based photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simple Ag-based compounds and their composites
ce:section.ce:para: Ag oxide (Ag2O) is a p-type semiconductor with narrow band gap. It possesses good responsive performance under visible light irradiation. The light absorption of Ag2O was mainly caused by the hybridization of O2p and Ag4d orbitals [38]. Previously, it was believed that Ag2O could absorb visible light, but it cannot be applied as photocatalyst because Ag2O has strong photosensitive property, which easily causes the decomposition of Ag2O in the photocatalytic reaction. Wang et al. [39] first found that Ag2O could be used as a visible light-driven photocatalyst and high self-stability and photocatalytic activity were obtained on it. Chen et al. [40] synthesized Ag2O photocatalyst by a hydrothermal method. It was observed that some Ag NPs adhered on the surface of Ag2O after 30min of photocatalytic reaction. The in situ generation of appropriate amount of Ag NPs could effectively promote the activity and stability, which was confirmed by the recycled reactions. Ag sulfide (Ag2S) is another n-type direct band semiconductor, which was widely applied in devices including luminescent materials, infra-red detector and photoconductors. Nano-sized Ag2S materials have more potential applications in light absorption, sensing and catalysis due to their large surface area and obvious quantum size effect [41]. However, the photocatalytic performance of pure Ag2S is not desirable. Therefore, Ag2S was usually combined with other compound and was applied as composite photocatalyst. Ag2S composites have obvious activity in photocatalytic production of hydrogen and the degradation of organic pollutants. For example, ZnS−In2S3−Ag2S synthesized by hydrothermal route can effectively decompose H2O and generate H2. The production rate of H2 reached 3.3mmol/h [42]. Xie et al. [43] prepared Ag2S−TiO2 composite nanostructure by a simple liquid phase method and found that the degradation rate of MO over Ag2S−TiO2 is about six times of that of P25-TiO2 under visible light irradiation.

Silver halides (AgX, XCl, Br, I) have strong visible light response property. But, silver halides are similar to Ag2O in photosensitive property, which causes the poor stability. Kuai et al. [44] first prepared AgBr, then under visible light irradiation, Ag/AgBr was produced. They proposed a photocatalytic mechanism for the high photocatalytic performance of Ag/AgBr. As indicated in Fig. 1 , under visible light irradiation, Ag NPs were produced in situ over the surface of AgBr via the reduction of Ag+ in AgBr by the photogenerated electrons (e−). The layer of Ag formed on the surface of AgBr could prevent the decomposition of AgBr, thus greatly improving the stability of AgBr. At the same time, Ag has good conductivity and the photogenerated electrons (e−) of AgBr could effectively be transferred to the Ag and the adsorbed O2 can instantaneously capture e− to produce O2 -, which suppresses the combination of e− and h+. On the other hand, h+ stays on the AgBr and Br− was oxidized by h+ into Br0. The organic pollutant was decomposed by both Br0 and h+ due to their powerful oxidation ability. It was found that exposure to sun light for 5min, methylene blue (MB) dye can be completely degraded by Ag/AgBr. More importantly, the activity remained almost unchanged after five times of recycling reactions.

The plasma effect of noble metals can be used to promote the photocatalytic performance [45,46], which is also effective for improving the performance of silver halide catalysts [47]. For example, the photocatalytic activity of Ag/AgCl [48], Ag/AgBr [49] and Ag/Ag(Br, I)[50] plasma systems were studied in detail. It was found that one of the obvious roles of Ag NPs is to enhance the absorption in the visible light region through surface plasmon resonance effect. In the plasmonic system of Ag/AgBr and Ag/AgCl, the photocatalytic efficiency of Ag/AgBr is much higher than that of Ag/AgCl, and Ag/Ag(Br, I) has a stronger photocatalytic reducibility than Ag/AgCl. Qi et al. [51] prepared another novel visible light responsive plasmonic Ag@AgCl composite by immobilizing AgCl NPs onto titanate nanowire films. The as-prepared films exhibited much higher visible light photocatalytic performance in degradation of methyl orange (MO), Rhodamine B (RhB) and phenol than that of P25 (TiO2).

The typical Ag oxacid salt semiconductors include Ag3AsO4, AgNbO3, AgAlO2, AgGaO2, AgInO3, AgSbO3, AgGeO3, AgCrO4, and etc. Among them, Ag3PO4 is well known as an excellent semiconductor photocatalyst in degrading organic pollutants and decomposing water. Ag3PO4 is a narrow band gap (E g =2.36∼2.42eV) semiconductor with a cubic crystal structure [52]. As shown in Fig. 2 , it is composed of PO4 and AgO4 tetrahedrons. Its oxidation potential of VB is close to 2.9eV. As a result, it has remarkable effect in some photocatalytic oxidation reactions under visible light irradiation, such as oxygen production from water and mineralization of organic pollutants [53]. At the same time, Ag+ in Ag3PO4 can combine with photo-generated electrons to produce Ag. The Ag/Ag3PO4 system could maintain the state of constant consumption electrons, which greatly benefits the holes production. Therefore, high efficiency of photocatalytic oxidation reaction could proceed.

Ag3PO4 can be fabricated by different methods, such as precipitation [54–57], ion exchange [58], hydrotherm [59], electrochemical oxidation [60], and etc. Bi et al. [54] prepared Ag3PO4 with size less than 1μm by liquid phase deposition. It confirmed that Ag3PO4 has a strong adsorption in visible light spectrum. Under visible light irradiation (> 420nm), the oxygen production of this Ag3PO4 could reach 636μmol/h. Moreover, this catalyst could keep high efficiency in degradation of organic pollutant. For example, the MB could be totally decomposed in 4min.

Wang et al. [61] prepared Ag3PO4 NPs through precipitation−displacement method. They found that 97% MO could be degraded within 60min under visible light irradiation and more than two times than TiO2-P25 in activity were observed. Ag3PO4 spheroidal particles were synthesizes by precipitation method and the stability of this catalyst was investigated by recycle experiment [62]. The Ag3PO4 spheroidal particles showed high stability. With the increase of recycle times, a small change was observed on the morphology, but photocatalytic performance displayed no obvious variation even after seven times recycles. Fig. 3 shows another porous Ag3PO4 microcubes prepared by ion exchange route [62]. These porous Ag3PO4 microcubes exhibited promoted photocatalytic activity with respect to common structure and commercial titanium dioxide.
Other Ag oxacid salt semiconductors, such as AgNbO3, AgAlO2, AgGaO2, AgInO3, AgSbO3, AgGeO3, and AgCrO4, were also intensively investigated in photolysis water to produce hydrogen [63–68].

In Ag-based solid solution semiconductor, the potentials position of valence band and conduction band can be regulated elaborately to realize the adjustment of the energy gap. For example, for β-AgAl1-x Ga x O2 photocatalyst [69], with the increase of x from 0 to 1, the band gap energy of the solid solution degreased gradually from 2.83 to 2.19eV. Correspondingly, the color of the sample changed slowly from gray to orange. The experimental results showed that, when x =0.4 (β-AgAl0.6Ga0.4O2), the apparent quantum efficiency of the solid solution in catalytic degradation of isopropyl alcohol was the largest (37.3%). The photocatalytic activity of β-AgAl0.6Ga0.4O2 was 35 and 63 times of that of pure β-AgAlO2 and β-AgGaO2, respectively.

Similarly, Tsuji et al. [70] successfully prepared (AgIn) x Zn(1-x)S2 solid solution. This solid solution photocatalyst can decompose water to product hydrogen under visible light irradiation. When the value of x was 0.22 and the band gap was 2.3eV, the best photocatalytic activity appeared. The apparent quantum efficiency reached 20% and hydrogen generation rate was 3.3L/(m2·h).

Another (AgNbO3)1-x (SrTiO3) x composite solid solution was reported by Wang et al. [71]. Compared with pure AgNbO2 and SrTiO3, the photocatalytic activity of (AgNbO3)1-x (SrTiO3) x increased significantly. With the value of x =0.25, (AgNbO3) 0.75(SrTiO3)0.25 displayed the best catalytic activity. In addition to the degradation of organic dyes, AgNbO3)0.75(SrTiO3)0.25 can also effectively decompose water to generate oxygen. Ag1-x Na x NbO3 is a similar silver-based solid solution [72], which has a good photocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Some strategies in design Ag-based semiconductor photocatalysts with high performance
ce:section.ce:para: From the former analysis, we can know that most of the Ag-based semiconductor photocatalysts possess a strong ability to absorb visible light and obvious photocatalytic activity could be obtained. However, at present, there are few practical applications of Ag-based photocatalysts in environmental purification. The main reason is due to their poor stability and high operation cost. As former mention, the major drawback of Ag-based semiconductors is low stability in their photocatalytic process due to photochemical corrosion. Moreover, the activity is expected to be further promoted to meet the requirement of practical application. Therefore, the promotion of the photocatalytic performance becomes an urgent task before putting Ag-based semiconductor photocatalysts into practical application. It is known that the optical, electrical, and catalytic properties of Ag-based semiconductors strongly depend on their crystal microstructures and morphologies at the nanometre scale. The main methods for improving the photocatalytic performance include former-mentioned plasma effect, design novel heterojunctions, morphology or crystal facet control and innovation in fabrication method.

Recent research indicated that the formation of well-defined heterojunction between two semiconductors with matching electronic band structure can effectively suppress the recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes [73,74]. In Ag-based semiconductors, one of the main reasons for the loss of activity is the excessive photoreduction of Ag+ (Ag+ +e− →Ag). Once the heterojunction is formed, the photogenerated e− can be quickly transferred, which could make Ag-based semiconductor avoid photocorrosion. Fig. 4 illustrates the role of the typical p−n heterojunction in enhancing the separation efficiency of e-−h+ pairs. In a semiconductor, as one part is doped with donor impurity, the n-type semiconductor can be produced, and another part is doped with acceptor impurity, becoming p-type semiconductor. The interface between the combined p- and n-type semiconductors is called p−n heterojunction. There exists strong local electric field nearby the interface of p−n heterojunction due to the existence of enriched negatively charged ions in p-type part and positively charged ions in n-type part. In the p−n heterojunction, the direction of electric field is from n toward p. The difference of electric potential in electric field can become the driving force for enhancing the separation of photo-generated electrons and holes, resulting in high quantum efficiency in photocatalytic reactions. As the result, the photo-induced electrons and holes are efficiency separated and extend the lifetime of photo-induced charge carriers, thus enhancing the photocatalytic performance of photocatalysts [75]. In factor, heterojunction is not limited to p- and n type semiconductor. Other two semiconductors with matching electronic band structure can form effective heterojunction for promoting the separation of photo-generated electrons and holes.

Ag-based heterojunction materials can be designed as one Ag-based semiconductor coupled with other Ag-based or other semiconductors. The following is a typical example of Ag2O/Ag2CO3 heterojunction photocatalyst [77]. Ag2O/Ag2CO3 heterojunction can be fabricated by a phase transformation route. The synthetic route is shown in Fig. 5a. In the process of calcination, the Ag2CO3 surface first breaks down into Ag2O and the generated Ag2O NPs coat over the surface of Ag2CO3, producing core−shell-like Ag2CO3/Ag2O heterojunction. The theoretical calculation shows that the band gap energies of Ag2CO3 and Ag2O were 2.5 and 1.3eV, respectively; the potentials of Ag2CO3 valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) were 2.77 and 0.27eV, respectively; the potentials of Ag2O VB and CB were 1.5 and 0.2eV. Fig. 5b displays the band structure of Ag2CO3 and Ag2O.

In this Ag2O/Ag2CO3 heterjunction system, Ag2O has more negative conduction band and valence band than Ag2CO3. Under visible light irradiation, the photo-produced electrons on the Ag2O can quickly move to the Ag2CO3 surface. At the same, the photo-produced holes on the Ag2CO3 surface can transfer to the surface of Ag2O, thus increasing the separation efficiency of electrons and holes for each other. The activity test showed that the activity of Ag2O/Ag2CO3 in photocatalytic degradation of MO was 67 and 31 times than Ag2CO3 and Ag2O, respectively. In addition, the stability test showed that in the second cycle reaction, Ag2CO3 and Ag2O almost lost the activity because of the silver generated due to photochemical etching. But even after six times cyclic reaction, Ag2O/Ag2CO3 can degradation of MO in 40min by degradation rate reached 94%.

Other AgX(XCl, Br, I)/Ag2CO3 heterojunction photocatalysts were fabricated by simple co-precipitation method [35,36]. It was found that the light absorption ability of AgX(XCl, Br, I)/Ag2CO3 heterojunction composites could be improved remarkably through coupling of AgX. Moreover, the photocatalytic performances of the composites were much higher than that of pure AgX and Ag2CO3. As shown in Table 2 , the MO degradation rate constant for the 20% AgI/Ag2CO3 catalyst (0.54h-1) is 2.6 times faster than that of Ag2CO3 (0.15h-1) and 71 times larger than AgI(0.03h-1). MO could be degraded completely over 20% AgI/Ag2CO3 within 12min under visible light. The insurmountable problem for silver-based semiconductor photocatalysts in photocatalytic process is their poor stability, which causes the difficulty of catalysts recycling. From Fig. 6 , we can see that relatively high stability was obtained over the 20% AgI/Ag2CO3. After five successive cycles, it still gives 30% degradation rate of MO after 5h visible light irradiation, indicating a large increase in stability was obtained. (Tables 3 and 4
)

Other Ag-based semiconductor heterojunction were also reported. Yan et al. [78] prepared AgI/Ag3PO4 heterojunction composites by ion exchange. This composite exhibited extremely strong light absorption capacity. Under visible light irradiation, the RhB degradation rate over 20% AgI/Ag3PO4 could reach 93.8% within 6min. However, the RhB degradation rates over pure AgI and Ag3PO4 are only 12.6 and 23.4%, respectively. Another Ag3PO4/TiO2 heterojunction photocatalyst was synthesized by in situ chemical precipitation method [79]. It was found that with respect to pure Ag3PO4, the degradation rate of MB for Ag3PO4/TiO2 increased 47.8%, and increased 116.7% for degrading RhB. Meanwhile, it is worth noting that the mass fraction of Ag in composite decreased from 77 (pure Ag3PO4) to 47%. Thus, the operation cost of the catalyst was largely reduced. Zhao et al. [80] developed a novel two-step strategy to synthesize AgCl-loaded mesoporous anatase TiO2 photocatalyst. The AgCl/TiO2 nanocomposites had mesoporous structure and large specific surface area (222.86m2 g-1). Compared with commercial P25 photocatalyst, AgCl/TiO2 nanocomposites exhibited superior photocatalytic activity in degradation of MO.

Besides the good degradation effect on organic pollutants, Ag-based heterojunction semiconductors have good sterilization. For instance, Hu et al. [81] prepared Ag/AgBr/TiO2 composite photocatalyst by deposition route. Under visible light irradiation, TiO2 and Ag/TiO2 have almost no activity in degrading azo-dyes and eliminating colibacillus. But Ag/AgBr/TiO2 can effectively degrade azo-dyes and eliminate colibacillus. More importantly, this heterojunction catalyst showed high stability. Using Ag8W4O16 and HBr as raw material, Huang et al. [82] prepared a new Ag/AgBr/WO3·3H2O composite catalyst which can effectively remove colibacillus. Wang et al. [83] successfully synthesized a natural sunlight-driven AgI/ZnO heterojunction by in situ deposition method. The results indicated that the AgI/ZnO composites displayed much higher photocatalytic performance than that of ZnO and AgI. The AgI/ZnO catalyst showed highly efficiency in the degradation of methylene blue (MB) and RhB under natural sunlight irradiation and nearly 100% dyes were degraded in 120min.

Other surface modification by various heterojunction or co-catalysts is effective to improve the performance of Ag-based semiconductors. Yu et al. [89] used an impregnation method to graft Fe(III) on the surface of AgBr particles to form Fe(III)/AgBr photocatalysts. It was found that the Fe(III) clusters could act as a new and effective co-catalyst to improve the photocatalytic activity and stability of AgBr photocatalyst. After surface coating by Fe(III) co-catalyst (8.2at%), the photocatalytic activity of AgBr photocatalyst can be largely proved by a factor of 73% even after five cycles of photocatalytic reactions. M/Ag3PO4 (MPt, Pd, Au) Schottky-type heterostructures were successfully fabricated by a chemical deposition route using NaBH4 as a reduction agent [90]. These noble metals NPS were highly dispersed on the surface of Ag3PO4 polyhedrons. The photocurrent response over M/Ag3PO4 electrodes was much higher than that of pure Ag3PO4 and follows the order of Pt>Pd>Au.

From the above, we can see that the surface modification by formation of heterojunction or co-catalysts in Ag-based semiconductor could not only improve photocatalytic performance but also overcome the poor stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology control of Ag-based semiconductor photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic performance is not only related to the energy structure of the semiconductor, but also closely related to the morphology and crystal property. Luo et al. [91] have reported a sphere-like AgCl crystal (Fig. 7 ). These sphere-like AgCl crystals were synthesized with the assistance of C x MimCl. C x MimCl was regarded to play an important role in the formation of sphere-like structure, which would finally lead to the formation of high index facets. Ag@AgCl synthesized from the near-spherical AgCl exhibits much higher photocatalytic activity than that with cubic morphology.

Ag2O microcrystals with different morphologies have been successfully synthesized by various complexing agents [92]. Complexing anions play an important role in the growth of the Ag2O microcrystals. The kinetic control leads to five morphologies of Ag2O microcrystals (cubic, octahedral, rhombic, dodecahedra, polyhedral with 18 faces and rhombicuboctahedra show in Fig. 8 ), which exhibit facet-dependent photocatalytic activity for the degradation of methyl orange (MO) under visible light irradiation.

From the reported results, it is known that most of the Ag-based semiconductors display small specific surface area and lack pore structure. The loading Ag-based semiconductor on the support can increase the specific surface area and improve the adsorption ability of reactants, thus promoting the catalytic reaction rate. With the help of the trisodium citrate (Na3Cit), Liang et al. [62] developed a one-step reaction method to fabricate the uniform hierarchical Ag3PO4 porous microcubes. It was found that Na3Cit played important roles as the structure directing agent, crystal growth modifier and aggregation-orienting agent for the formation of this unique porous Ag3PO4 microstructure. These Ag3PO4 porous microcubes display a broad pore-size distribution around 53nm. The specific surface area of the Ag3PO4 porous microcubes was calculated to be about 5.6m2 g-1, which was much higher than that of solid Ag3PO4 microcrystals (less than 1.0m2 g-1). A relatively large surface area and porous architecture of Ag3PO4 provides more active sites and diffusion pathways for adsorption and transportation of reactants, which would be beneficial to the photocatalytic process. In fact, the obtained Ag3PO4 porous microcubes showed remarkable enhanced visible light photocatalytic degradations of an aqueous solution of rhodamine B (RhB), far exceeding that of solid Ag3PO4 sample and commercial P25 powders.

High surface area hydroxyapatites have been explored as supports to load Ag3PO4 nanoparticles for antibacterial applications [95]. Hydroxyapatites stabilize sub-3nm pure Ag3PO4 nanoparticles. The hydroxyapatite-supported Ag3PO4 showed high bacterial kill rates. Applied apatite as the support, Elahifard [96] prepared the Ag/AgBr/TiO2 composite materials. With respect to unsupported Ag/TiO2 and Ag/AgBr/TiO2, under visible light irradiation, the Ag/AgBr/TiO2 supported over apatite exhibited significantly efficient in removing and inhibiting the growth of bacteria. Other supported Ag-based semiconductors were also reported. Hu et al. [97,98] developed a series of silver halides loaded in the mesoporous materials. Fig. 9 displays TEM images of mesoporous γ-Al2O3 supported Ag−AgBr fabricated by the deposition−precipitation method with surfactant P123 (Ag−AgBr/MAP) or without surfactant (Ag−AgBr/MA). It was found that Ag/AgI and Ag/AgBr can be highly dispersed in the ordered mesopores of γ-Al2O3 (MA). The dispersed Ag/AgI and Ag/AgBr displayed high efficiency and stability in decomposing organic pollutants (2-chlorophenol, 2,4-dichlorophenol, trichlorophenol) under visible light or sunlight irradiation.

Xu et al. [99] fabricated the spherical Bi2MoO6 nanoarchitectures as support to load Ag3PO4. Ag3PO4 NPs were then deposited onto the surface of Bi2MoO6 via deposition−precipitation technique. Under visible light, Ag3PO4/Bi2MoO6 nanocomposite displayed a much higher rate for degradation of RhB and MB than the pure Ag3PO4 NPs and Bi2MoO6. The observed improvement in photocatalytic activity was attributed to the extended absorption in the visible light region resulting from the Ag3PO4 NPs, and the effective separation of photogenerated carriers at the Ag3PO4/Bi2MoO6 interfaces.

Carbon materials possess some unique properties, such as large specific surface area, strong light absorption, safety, corrosion resistance, and excellent electrical conductivity. Previous studies have shown that in photocatalysis process, the electron-accepting and -transport properties of carbon nanomaterials provide a convenient way to direct the flow of photogenerated charge carriers; thus, enhanced photocatalytic performance can be achieved through the construction of semiconductor/carbon composites [100–103]. These unique properties of carbon nanomaterials are also effective in enhancing the performance of Ag-based semiconductors. Some typical carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [104], carbon nano-tubes [105], carbon nanofibers (CNF) [106], graphene [107–116] and etc., could act as good supports or an electron reservoir to hinder the electron/hole pair recombination probability for semiconductor photocatalysts.

CQDs are a novel class of recently discovered nanocarbons. They can display abundant photo-physical properties. Especially, their strong size and excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behaviors further enhance their photocatalytic properties. For example, Zhang et al. [104] prepared rhombic dodecahedral CQDs/Ag3PO4 composite photocatalysts by simple precipitation (shown in Fig. 10 ). The results of photocatalytic degradation of MO indicated that CQDs/Ag3PO4 had a good photocatalytic activity. MO was completed degraded within 10min under visible light irradiation. More importantly, catalyst can maintain good stability. Yu et al. [106] fabricated a novel Ag−AgI−TiO2/CNFs photocatalyst, in which Ag−AgI−TiO2 nanoparticles were embedded onto carbon nanofibers (CNF). The as-fabricated Ag−AgI−TiO2/CNFs showed high efficient light absorption and photocatalytic activity in decomposition of MO, acid red 18 (AR18), MB and fluorescence sodium under visible light irradiation.

In 2004, Novoselov et al. [108] found a new carbon nanomaterial-graphene (GR), which has become research hotspot in material, chemical and other fields [109–111]. Graphene is composed of carbon atom in single-layer with two-dimensional planar structure of hexagonal configuration [112], possessing much outstanding performance including large specific area, good electronic conductivity and stability [113,114]. Zhao et al. [115] modified TiO2 with GR and obtained GR/TiO2 composite photocatalyst. Its physical property and photocatalytic performance in degrading MB were investigated. It was observed that TiO2 in the composite existed as anatase phase and GR/TiO2 possessed better photocatalytic performance than that of TiO2 which had excellent performance.

Other carbon materials are graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (RGO). GO is one kind of monolithic derivatives of graphene [116]. Chen et al. [117] synthesized Ag3PO4/GO composite photocatalyst by liquid deposition, and photocatalytic degradation MO and RhB under visible light was investigated. They found that Ag3PO4 particles could be evenly distributed on the surface of GO layer. Ag3PO4/GO displayed good light absorption ability. When the loaded GO was 15% (mass concentration), 86.7% MO was degraded within 50min and RhB can be completely degraded within 30min. In particularly, the superior hydrophilicity of GO not only makes GO-modified photocatalyst easily scatter in many solvents to form stable suspension, but also could simplify the preparation of GO-based composite materials in solution. Liu et al. [118] fabricated a novel GO-enwrapped Ag3PO4 (GO−Ag3PO4) composite photocatalyst by ion-exchange method. They found that compared with Ag3PO4, the photocatalytic activity and stability of GO−Ag3PO4 were remarkably improved. At the same time, the composite material showed excellent antibacterial antivirus performance. At room temperature, Reddy et al. [119] used a facile template-free ultrasound-assisted method to fabricate novel RGO-enwrapped AgI nanocomposites. AgI−RGO nanocomposite exhibited much higher photocatalytic activity than the pure AgI. Moreover, with the assistance of H2O2, the photocatalytic activity of AgI−RGO can be further enhanced.

It should be noted that graphite-like C3N4 (g-C3N4) materials have similar structure to graphene and they could display obvious effect on enhancing the performance of Ag-based semiconductors. Xiu et al. [120] developed a facile chemisorption method to fabricate g-C3N4-modified Ag3PO4 nanoparticles. The g-C3N4-modified Ag3PO4 nanoparticles exhibited enhanced photocatalytic activities under visible light irradiation (λ≥400). The enhanced photocatalytic activities were mainly ascribed to the synergistic effect between g-C3N4 and Ag3PO4, which led to an improved separation efficiency of electron−hole pairs.
From the above analysis, we can see that Ag-based semiconductors coupled with carbon materials could not only improve the photocatalytic activity of silver compounds but also promote the stability.

Ag-based semiconductors are interesting as visible-driven photocatalytic materials due to their good visible light response performance. High initial photocatalytic activity could be obtained over most of Ag-based semiconductors. Their performance is closely related to their crystal microstructures, morphologies and texture properties. But in practical application as photocatalyst, the main disadvantage of Ag-based semiconductors is not stable and easy to take place photochemical corrosion. Moreover, small specific surface area and the lack of pore structure are also adverse in photocatalytic reaction. By designing heterostructure, producing plasmonic effect, and coupling carbon materials in Ag-based semiconductor, the photocatalytic activity and stability of the catalyst could be increased. Moreover, through the innovation of the catalyst preparation method, Ag-based semiconductor photocatalyst can be endowed rich pore structure and high specific surface area, which also can effectively enhance the photocatalytic performance. In addition, in order to improve the catalytic performance, we can develop Ag-based crystals with specific morphology or exposed crystal facets. Ag-based semiconductors with unique morphology or special exposed crystal facets could largely promote the photocatalytic behavior.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21067004, 21263005), Yangfan Project of Guangdong Province, the Young Science and Technology Project of Jiangxi Province Natural Science Foundation China (20133BAB21003), The Landing Project of Science and Technology of Colleges and Universities in Jiangxi Province (KJLD14046), Young Scientist Training Project of Jiangxi Province (20122BCB23015), Yuanhang Engineering of Jiangxi Province, Graduate innovation project of Jiangxi Province (3104000089, 3104100013) and Graduate innovation project of Jiangxi University of Science and Technology (3104100039).
